elements.sent-2411-033: Prior to LLMs, the approaches to retrieval of information from unstructured text included statistical and machine learning-based approaches.
elements.sent-2411-034: Examples of statistical approaches include bag-of-words and term frequency-inverse document frequency (TF-IDF) \cite{ramos2003using .}
elements.sent-2411-035: For instance, the TF-IDF approach has been used for extracting ontological data from unstructured text \cite{berndt2010using .}
elements.sent-2411-036: On the other hand, machine learning approaches are based on generating word embeddings such as Word2vec \cite{mikolov2013efficient , which captures the idea of similarity between words within documents.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Recently, new technologies have emerged, such as large language models that generate new text and embeddings from existing documents.
elements.sent-2411-038: Although very promising in their capabilities, these models can suffer from hallucinations, generating responses that are not grounded in facts.
elements.sent-2411-039: LLMs come up with their responses mainly based on next-word probability \cite{wolfram2023chatgpt .}
elements.sent-2411-040: These hallucinatory tendencies impede the application of LLMs to mainstream industry applications because of obvious questions about the reliability and robustness of their responses \cite{lewis2020retrieval \cite{bang2023multitask .}}
elements.sent-2411-041: To address this, a lot of research has been published that suggests a variety of approaches which are summarized in table \ref{tab:long-lit.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Figure \ref{fig:llm provides an overview of the LLM landscape highlighting the use of both proprietary ($A$) and public ($B$) documents.}
elements.sent-2411-043: An LLM is typically trained on vast amounts of public data but can be customized by additional training on proprietary documents (using full or parameter-efficient fine-tuning).
elements.sent-2411-044: There could even be an overlap between proprietary and public documents ($A \cap B$) if private documents are released or public documents are retracted.
elements.sent-2411-045: Of course, the documents generated by an LLM can be re-cycled as training data as well.
elements.sent-2411-046: Using an LLM involves prompt engineering with or without examples, which can be drawn from seen or unseen documents.
elements.sent-2411-047: Several possible approaches to reduce hallucinations are outlined below.
elements.sent-2411-048: Full fine-tuning: The pre-trained LLM understands the basic language construct, but it is not adapted for domain-specific context and vocabulary.
elements.sent-2411-049: This approach basically modifies the LLM's weights to adopt the context from the domain-specific dataset.
elements.sent-2411-050: It is one of the oldest approaches for domain-specific tuning of an LLM.
elements.sent-2411-051: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning: In this approach, the LLM is fine-tuned by modifying only a small number of parameters \cite{hu2021lora \cite{lester2021power rather than all of them, as in the case of full fine-tuning.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: This approach reduces the load with respect to computations, memory use and execution time.
elements.sent-2411-053: Prompt engineering: Prompt engineering is an approach where the LLM is given instructions to generate a certain response.
elements.sent-2411-054: Different approaches have been shown to enhance the quality of responses generated using prompt engineering.
elements.sent-2411-055: Some of them include zero-shot prompting \cite{kojima2022large , where the model is given only instructions but no examples, few-shot prompting where the model is given instructions and a few examples \cite{parnami2022learning , chain-of-thought prompting \cite{wei2022chain where the model is guided through steps for problem-solving, ReAct \cite{yao2022react where the model is guided using reasoning and task-specific actions in an interleaved manner, or some combination of these different approaches.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is an approach that enhances the capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) by augmenting their prompts with information retrieved from a knowledge base that the LLM has not seen during training.
elements.sent-2411-057: This method adds domain-specific context to the generated responses, leveraging the LLM's ability to generate text while incorporating embeddings from existing documents stored in vector databases.
elements.sent-2411-058: These databases are specifically designed to store, query, and retrieve sentence embeddings, making them integral to the RAG approach.

elements.sent-2411-077: The advent of large language models (LLMs) like ChatGPT has revolutionized the way information can be retrieved from proprietary documents.
elements.sent-2411-078: However, given the relative immaturity of these models, their integration into socio-technical systems must be meticulously designed to ensure that the final solution is both reliable and accurate.
elements.sent-2411-079: This design process involves extensive experimentation with prompt engineering, innovative strategies for data chunking, indexing, and retrieval, as well as iterative and thorough testing of the entire system.
elements.sent-2411-080: In this section, we explore the challenges and opportunities of implementing RAG systems in production.
elements.sent-2411-081: We outline the key technological decisions required when designing such a system, focusing on the various components of RAG.
elements.sent-2411-082: We begin by discussing the need for and background of developing an RAG-based system for information retrieval in our field study, highlighting the main pain points in the existing system and the areas where RAG with LLMs was evaluated to add value.
elements.sent-2411-083: We then detail our design process, emphasizing our approach in light of user readiness for this human-AI system.
elements.sent-2411-084: In our field study, focused on developing a pilot solution for an IT product company, the challenge of integrating LLMs like ChatGPT into socio-technical systems became particularly relevant.
elements.sent-2411-085: The variability of product versions across different customer environments in IT companies demands a trained support team capable of delivering prompt and accurate assistance.
elements.sent-2411-086: Addressing this need required a carefully designed system involving extensive experimentation with prompt engineering, innovative data chunking, and indexing methods, as well as iterative testing and refinement to ensure the reliability and accuracy of the LLM-based solution.
elements.sent-2411-087: In IT organizations, support is often structured in multiple tiers, including internal and customer-facing support.
elements.sent-2411-088: Although the initial design of our system was not meant for direct customer access, it was essential to develop it in a way that allows customer support teams to efficiently retrieve the necessary information to address customer inquiries and escalations.
elements.sent-2411-089: As the system's reliability and accuracy are validated, there may be potential to explore its direct use by end customers.
elements.sent-2411-090: In Figure \ref{fig:domain , we identify key problem areas in the current state.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{ProblemContext.eps} \caption{Domain Background Analysis - Identification of Problem areas in the space} \label{fig:domain}
elements.sent-2411-091: The first pain point is the frequent back-and-forth communication between the customer support team and the engineering team, which is time-consuming and disruptive.
elements.sent-2411-092: This challenge stems from the need for customer support teams to meet Service Level Agreements (SLAs) by providing timely and accurate responses to customer tickets.
elements.sent-2411-093: Ensuring the accuracy of responses requires verification across various product documents, which differ by product and version.
elements.sent-2411-094: By improving the efficiency of information retrieval from these documents, these pain points can be mitigated, enabling support teams to meet SLAs more effectively.
elements.sent-2411-095: The second pain point is the fragmented nature of information sources within most organizations.
elements.sent-2411-096: Information is often spread across multiple knowledge management systems, ticketing systems, databases, and other formats, each in its own silo.
elements.sent-2411-097: The integration of information across these disparate systems is complex and presents a significant challenge.
elements.sent-2411-098: In most organizations, retrieving information is not just about obtaining data; it’s about doing so quickly, accurately, and in a user-friendly manner.
elements.sent-2411-099: The ideal solution would allow for natural language queries without requiring users to have expertise in complex querying languages like SQL.
elements.sent-2411-100: Additionally, the information retrieved should be easy to understand or even summarized for the user’s convenience.
elements.sent-2411-101: A significant challenge lies in the evolution of knowledge management systems alongside the organization itself.
elements.sent-2411-102: This evolution often results in inconsistencies in data formats, with information being both structured and unstructured.
elements.sent-2411-103: Organizations typically have a wide variety of document formats, such as .doc, .docx, .pdf, .csv, .xls, .txt, and HTML, among others.
elements.sent-2411-104: Furthermore, this information is often scattered across multiple systems, including various knowledge management frameworks, ticketing systems, and databases, each siloed with its own format.
elements.sent-2411-105: Integrating this diverse information into a cohesive system is inherently complex and requires a nuanced approach.
elements.sent-2411-106: Beyond these technical challenges, several external factors influence the adoption and implementation of emergent technologies like Large Language Models (LLMs):.
elements.sent-2411-107: Legal and governance: Many organizations are cautious about exposing proprietary documents to external LLMs due to internal legal and compliance requirements.
elements.sent-2411-108: This includes concerns about data security and the risks associated with hosting sensitive information outside the organization’s infrastructure or sharing it with third-party vendors.
elements.sent-2411-109: Financing: Budgetary constraints often dictate whether an organization can implement a solution in-house or must opt for outsourcing.
elements.sent-2411-110: While outsourcing might provide a ready-to-use solution, it often comes with recurring subscription costs that can strain organizational budgets.
elements.sent-2411-111: On the other hand, in-house development requires a team with specialized skills for ongoing development, maintenance, and support.
elements.sent-2411-112: Volatility: The landscape of LLM technologies is rapidly evolving, with new libraries and frameworks emerging frequently.
elements.sent-2411-113: This volatility presents challenges not only for in-house development but also when negotiating contracts with third-party vendors.
elements.sent-2411-114: Organizations must carefully consider the total cost of ownership, including the long-term costs associated with keeping the system up-to-date.
elements.sent-2411-115: When developing a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system, particularly when using Large Language Models (LLMs) with proprietary documents, organizations must establish clear functional, non-functional, and technical requirements.
elements.sent-2411-116: In our fieldwork, we began this process by understanding the specific needs and expectations of stakeholders, including end-users, developers, and business leaders.
elements.sent-2411-117: The functional requirements focus on the system’s core capabilities, such as natural language understanding, contextual awareness, and integration with existing systems.
elements.sent-2411-118: The non-functional requirements, on the other hand, emphasize performance aspects like scalability, security, and reliability, ensuring the system can operate efficiently under varying conditions.
elements.sent-2411-119: Technical requirements detail the necessary infrastructure, tools, and frameworks that support the system’s development and operation.
elements.sent-2411-120: Stakeholder requirements and acceptance criteria further refine the project by aligning the system’s features with user needs and setting clear benchmarks for success.
elements.sent-2411-121: Throughout this process, involving key teams—such as legal, compliance, engineering, customer support, and quality assurance—ensured that the system not only met technical and functional standards but also adhered to organizational policies and regulations.
elements.sent-2411-122: During the initial phase, we conducted feasibility checks to define the scope of a Minimum Viable Product (MVP) and to plan a roadmap for incremental development.
elements.sent-2411-123: This approach is crucial as it ensures that the system design remains flexible enough to evolve over time, allowing for adjustments as the technology and organizational needs change.
elements.sent-2411-124: Feasibility analysis and careful planning are critical to the overall success of the project, ensuring that the final product meets both the technical and business requirements of the organization.
elements.sent-2411-125: To ensure the solution met user needs, we assembled a cross-functional team to collaboratively design the system.
elements.sent-2411-126: This approach enabled users to actively participate not only in defining requirements and conducting final acceptance testing but also in the iterative development and evaluation phases.
elements.sent-2411-127: By involving users throughout the process, the design was continuously refined, aligning more closely with their expectations and operational realities.
elements.sent-2411-128: To assess user readiness and the acceptance of the new generative AI-based Information Retrieval System, we distributed a pre-survey questionnaire among the pilot group of users selcted randomly across various departments.
elements.sent-2411-129: This survey was a key step in our process, as it aimed to capture the users' familiarity with generative AI, their expectations, potential concerns, and overall sentiment towards the new technology.

elements.sent-2411-141: About 8\% of users were uncertain about what data the AI should provide, indicating a need for clearer guidelines on the system's capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-142: General Sentiment: Overall, the sentiment was positive, with 25\% of users expressing excitement and gratitude for being part of the pilot program.
elements.sent-2411-143: This enthusiasm will likely aid in the system’s adoption, provided that their concerns are adequately addressed through training and iterative improvements.
elements.sent-2411-144: This pre-survey analysis underscores the importance of addressing user concerns and managing expectations while leveraging the enthusiasm and existing familiarity with generative AI to facilitate a smooth rollout of the new system.
elements.sent-2411-145: In the development of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, selecting the right model, vector database, and cloud provider is critical to ensuring optimal performance and alignment with business objectives.
elements.sent-2411-146: The landscape of available technologies is vast, with each offering unique features, scalability options, and integration capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-147: For instance, different LLMs vary in their context windows, multimodal capabilities, and accessibility, influencing their suitability for specific use cases.
elements.sent-2411-148: Similarly, the choice of vector databases, directly impacts the system's ability to efficiently manage and search through large datasets.
elements.sent-2411-149: Furthermore, the cloud provider must be carefully chosen to support the desired scalability, security, and compliance needs.
elements.sent-2411-150: Each of these decisions must be tailored to the specific requirements of the business case, as the right combination can significantly enhance the effectiveness of the RAG system while ensuring it meets the operational demands and strategic goals of the organization.
elements.sent-2411-151: In the following subsections, we explore the available options for each of these key components of the RAG system.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Algorithm for Retrieval Augmented Generation using LLM}\label{alg:rag-llm} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Create a prompt template with basic instructions to the LLM and include placeholders for retrieved document, user query and chat history \State Instantiate splitter to chunk documents \State docs = Split documents using splitter \State Instantiate embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings() \State Instantiate Vector Database Chroma to store the split documents "docs" and their embeddings \State Specify the vector database search type and number of top matching results for retrieval \State Instantiate retriever \State Save vector database in a persistent directory \While{Chat session not exited} \State Get the query from the application's UI \State Generate Embeddings for the query \State Search top 3 results from the vector database that have embeddings "similar" to the query embeddings \State Update the prompt template with the query and retrieved document and the chat history \State Send the prompt to the LLM ChatGPT API \State Receive the response from the ChatGPT API \State Save information on query, retrieved document titles and response in a log file \State Reformat the response for presentation to the user via UI \EndWhile \end{algorithmic}

If there isn't enough information below, say you don't know. Do not generate answers that don't use the sources below. For tabular information return it as an HTML table. elements.sent-2411-242: Incorporating robust logging mechanisms is essential for the effective operation and optimization of LLM-based systems.
elements.sent-2411-243: Logging every input and output interaction with the LLM is not just a best practice for troubleshooting; it is also vital for fine-tuning the model over time.
elements.sent-2411-244: Tracking the number of tokens in each input, context, and response—whether the LLM is hosted locally or accessed via an API—provides valuable insights.
elements.sent-2411-245: This data is crucial for planning infrastructure requirements and ensuring the scalability of the system, particularly as traffic increases.
elements.sent-2411-246: Logging also aids in understanding the model's behavior, identifying patterns of errors or inefficiencies, and making informed decisions about future adjustments to the system architecture.
elements.sent-2411-247: For the semantic search and retrieval component of our system, we conducted an extensive evaluation of various vector stores, both open-source and commercial, including Chroma, Pinecone, and ElasticSearch.
elements.sent-2411-248: A key finding from our evaluation was that misalignments between the generated responses and the user's queries often stemmed from issues in the retrieval process rather than the LLM itself.
elements.sent-2411-249: To address this, we implemented functionality to display links to the documents retrieved during the search.
elements.sent-2411-250: This approach not only provided users with citations for fact-checking but also allowed us to diagnose and refine our retrieval strategies when incorrect documents were retrieved.
elements.sent-2411-251: We also implement relevance-checking approaches, which we will discuss in the evaluation section.
elements.sent-2411-252: Initially, we chunked documents without overlap, but further experimentation revealed that overlapping chunks improved retrieval accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-253: We also varied the number of retrieved results and tested different retrieval strategies, including similarity search, maximum marginal relevance, and hybrid approaches that combine keyword search with semantic retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-254: This iterative process helped us identify the optimal configuration for our specific documents and use case.
elements.sent-2411-255: Recent research underscores the effectiveness of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) approaches, particularly when combining semantic retrieval from vector databases with other techniques like knowledge graphs or fine-tuning strategies.
elements.sent-2411-256: Knowledge graphs offer a complementary retrieval method, storing and querying information in the form of entity-attribute-attribute value triples.
elements.sent-2411-257: However, generating knowledge graphs from proprietary documents poses significant challenges, especially when dealing with inconsistently structured documents that have evolved over time within various departments of an organization.
elements.sent-2411-258: Fine-tuning, including Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT), also presents challenges.
elements.sent-2411-259: While PEFT is less resource-intensive than traditional fine-tuning, it still requires creating a training set, training the model, and evaluating its performance.
elements.sent-2411-260: This process can be time-consuming, making it slower to develop compared to simply storing embeddings in vector databases.
elements.sent-2411-261: Nonetheless, research \cite{liu2022few indicates that combining fine-tuning with data augmentation can enhance retrieval accuracy, making it a viable option depending on the available resources, budget, and timeline.}
elements.sent-2411-262: Incorporating quality assurance and testing from the early stages of product development is essential, especially when dealing with advanced technologies like LLMs and semantic retrieval, which differ significantly from traditional keyword search systems.
elements.sent-2411-263: The testing strategies, cases, and plans must be adapted to address the unique challenges posed by LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-264: Additionally, evaluating the sociotechnical aspects of the system through user surveys provides valuable insights into the effectiveness and acceptance of the technology.
elements.sent-2411-265: To assess the qualitative aspects of the application, we conducted pre- and post-surveys with identified pilot users.
elements.sent-2411-266: The pre-survey (Appendix \ref{sec:presurvey ), discussed earlier, was aimed at evaluating user readiness and expectations.}
elements.sent-2411-267: After the pilot run, a post-survey (Appendix \ref{sec:postsurvey ) was conducted to gather feedback on the application's performance, user satisfaction, and areas for improvement.}

elements.sent-2411-269: Testing LLM-based systems necessitates a distinct approach from traditional software testing methodologies.
elements.sent-2411-270: Establishing a ground truth dataset is crucial for assessing the semantic accuracy of the generated responses.
elements.sent-2411-271: The testing process must be thorough, addressing both the generative and retrieval components of the system.
elements.sent-2411-272: When evaluating applications developed for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with Large Language Models (LLMs) on proprietary documents, organizations should implement a robust testing strategy to ensure the accuracy, reliability, and adherence to organizational standards.
elements.sent-2411-274: Ground Truth Comparison Develop a robust ground truth dataset consisting of correct and validated responses for a set of queries relevant to the proprietary documents.
elements.sent-2411-275: This dataset serves as a benchmark to evaluate the performance of the RAG application.
elements.sent-2411-276: Regularly compare the outputs of the RAG system against the ground truth dataset to assess the accuracy of the generated responses.
elements.sent-2411-277: Focus on key metrics such as precision, recall, and relevance.
elements.sent-2411-278: Scenario-Based Testing Create test scenarios that reflect real-world use cases, ensuring that the RAG application can correctly interpret and respond to complex queries within the appropriate context.
elements.sent-2411-279: Fact-Checking Implement automated fact-checking mechanisms to verify that the RAG system's generated content accurately reflects the source documents.
elements.sent-2411-280: One method could include embedding links to the original documents to aid in verification.
elements.sent-2411-281: Consistency To assess the system's consistency in responding to similar or related queries and to prevent contradictory outputs, an external LLM—often referred to as 'LLM-as-a-judge' \cite{zheng2024judging —can be utilized.}

elements.sent-2411-283: Based on these evaluations, we were able to adjust parameters such as temperature, top-p, frequency penalty, and presence penalty.

elements.sent-2411-313: This approach involves deliberately challenging the system with edge cases, adversarial prompts, or scenarios designed to provoke biased, harmful, or toxic responses.

elements.sent-2411-315: To implement red teaming effectively, organizations can deploy a secondary LLM, known as an adversarial LLM, to generate challenging inputs.
elements.sent-2411-316: This adversarial LLM can be configured to explore the limits of the primary system's capabilities, testing its response to extreme or unexpected situations.
elements.sent-2411-317: For example, it might introduce queries that push the boundaries of the system's ethical guidelines or attempt to elicit biased or misleading information.
elements.sent-2411-318: The insights gained from red teaming are invaluable for refining the system's safeguards, ensuring that it can handle a wide range of real-world scenarios without compromising on ethical standards or data integrity.
elements.sent-2411-319: Additionally, this process can help in identifying areas where further training or reinforcement of the LLM is necessary, contributing to the development of a more resilient and trustworthy AI system.
elements.sent-2411-320: Regular red teaming exercises, combined with iterative improvements based on the findings, are essential for maintaining the long-term security and reliability of RAG systems in any operational environment.

elements.sent-2411-332: As artificial intelligence (AI) advances, the importance of establishing robust governance frameworks and best practices becomes increasingly critical.
elements.sent-2411-333: We review previous research that highlights this necessity \cite{taeihagh2021governance \cite{butcher2019state \cite{dafoe2018ai , which primarily addresses global issues concerning the development, deployment, and regulation of AI, including its intended purposes and limitations.}}}
elements.sent-2411-334: Additionally, research has explored AI governance at the organizational level, which we use as the basis and adopt the definition stated \cite{mantymaki2022defining : "AI governance is a system of rules, practices, processes, and technological tools designed to ensure that an organization’s use of AI technologies aligns with its strategies, objectives, and values; complies with legal requirements; and adheres to the ethical AI principles upheld by the organization." Building on this definition and insights from our pilot study, we propose an AI governance model, particularly for emerging technologies such as Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG).}
elements.sent-2411-335: RAG represents a significant advancement in AI, enabling large language models (LLMs) to produce more accurate and contextually relevant outputs by integrating external data sources that are not part of the original training data.
elements.sent-2411-336: However, the implementation of RAG in real-world scenarios presents unique challenges, particularly when transitioning from research concepts to industry applications.
elements.sent-2411-337: In this section, we share the intersection of AI governance and the practical deployment of RAG, drawing on insights from this field study.
elements.sent-2411-338: Here, we share the importance of AI governance in ensuring that RAG implementations are not only effective but also aligned with ethical standards, regulatory requirements, and organizational goals.
elements.sent-2411-339: The study underscores the complexities involved in adopting RAG technology, including the need for stringent data management practices, transparency in AI decision-making, and the continuous monitoring of AI systems to mitigate risks such as bias, misinformation, and unintended consequences.
elements.sent-2411-340: From what we have learned, we provide a foundation for developing best practices that organizations can adopt to navigate the challenges of implementing RAG.
elements.sent-2411-341: These best practices address key areas such as data governance, system design, user engagement, and ongoing evaluation, all of which are essential for maximizing the benefits of RAG while minimizing potential pitfalls.
elements.sent-2411-342: By sharing these insights, we aim to contribute to the broader discourse on AI governance and offer actionable guidance for organizations looking to harness the power of RAG in a responsible and sustainable manner.
elements.sent-2411-343: In our field study, we developed an AI governance model designed to address the unique challenges posed by AI systems, particularly those involving Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG).
elements.sent-2411-344: Our proposed governance model includes both enhancements to existing governance frameworks and the introduction of novel governance practices specifically tailored to AI technologies.
elements.sent-2411-345: The model is divided into four broad categories: Architectural and Technological Governance, Risk Governance, Humanitarian Governance, and Production Governance.
elements.sent-2411-346: Given the distinct differences between AI systems and traditional information systems, significant updates to architectural and technological governance are required: Architectural Review Board For AI implementations, the Architectural Review Board must establish processes to differentiate between cloud-based and on-premise infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-347: It is essential to make informed decisions regarding the use of on-premise AI models versus API-based AI models, considering factors such as data security, performance, and scalability.
elements.sent-2411-348: Standard and Procedural Governance This aspect covers the development and enforcement of standards, guidelines, and best practices for integrating AI capabilities within both existing and new applications.
elements.sent-2411-349: Prompt Engineering Governance The effectiveness of an AI system, particularly those leveraging large language models (LLMs), heavily depends on the clarity, structure, and flexibility of the prompts used to guide the model's responses.
elements.sent-2411-350: With LLMs, prompt engineering lies at the heart of the system.
elements.sent-2411-351: Therefore, it is important to discuss this new governance for prompt engineering in more detail to ensure consistency, reusability, and maintainability of these prompts.
elements.sent-2411-352: Structuring the prompt templates through standardization is crucial for maintaining consistency across different applications and teams.
elements.sent-2411-353: These templates should be designed to be adaptable, allowing for easy customization while maintaining a core structure that ensures reliability.
elements.sent-2411-354: In addition, using a modular design, such as breaking the prompt into sections for prefix, context, instructions, and output format, allows teams to reuse and repurpose different sections of the prompt across various scenarios, enhancing efficiency and reducing the need for constant re-engineering.
elements.sent-2411-355: To enable reusability and flexibility, we propose establishing a library of prompt templates, thus enablimg teams to access a repository of well-tested prompts that can be adapted for various use cases.
elements.sent-2411-356: This reduces development time and ensures that best practices are consistently applied across the organization.
elements.sent-2411-357: To manage the evolution of prompt templates, version control should be implemented.
elements.sent-2411-358: This allows teams to track changes, revert to previous versions if necessary, and ensure that updates are systematically applied across all relevant systems.
elements.sent-2411-359: Version control also facilitates collaboration, enabling multiple teams to contribute to and refine prompt templates over time.
elements.sent-2411-360: While standardization is important, prompts must also be flexible enough to adapt to specific contexts or scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-361: Governance should include guidelines on how to modify prompts for different applications while maintaining the core principles of clarity, accuracy, and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-362: For maintenance and continuous improvement, prompt templates should undergo regular audits to ensure they remain effective and aligned with the latest developments in AI technology and organizational needs.
elements.sent-2411-363: These audits should assess the performance of prompts, identifying areas for improvement and updating templates accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-364: Establishing feedback mechanisms is crucial for the continuous improvement of prompt engineering.
elements.sent-2411-365: Teams should collect and analyze feedback from end-users and developers to refine prompts and address any issues that arise during deployment.
elements.sent-2411-366: This iterative process helps to keep prompts relevant and effective in changing environments.
elements.sent-2411-367: In addition, comprehensive training and documentation are essential for ensuring that all team members understand how to create, use, and maintain prompt templates.
elements.sent-2411-368: Documentation should cover the principles of prompt engineering, provide examples of best practices, and offer guidance on troubleshooting common issues.
elements.sent-2411-369: Finally, effective prompt engineering governance requires input from various disciplines, including AI specialists, domain experts, and end-users.
elements.sent-2411-370: This cross-functional collaboration approach ensures that prompts are not only technically sound but also aligned with the specific needs of the application and the users it serves.
elements.sent-2411-371: Prompt engineering governance should not be the sole responsibility of a single team.
elements.sent-2411-372: Instead, it should be a shared responsibility across the organization, with clear roles and responsibilities assigned to different teams.
elements.sent-2411-373: This approach ensures that prompt engineering remains a priority and that best practices are consistently applied across all projects.
elements.sent-2411-374: CI-CD (Continuous Improvement / Continuous Development) Governance The rapidly changing landscape of AI technology necessitates a reevaluation of existing CI-CD practices.
elements.sent-2411-375: Organizations must adjust their frequency of data and code updates to stay aligned with the latest advancements in AI vendor technologies.
elements.sent-2411-376: Additionally, changes in laws and regulations affecting AI implementations must be incorporated into these processes.
elements.sent-2411-377: Risk governance provides oversight to assess and mitigate the potential risks associated with AI implementations, ensuring that they do not adversely impact the organization’s reputation or operational integrity: Technology Risk Due to the emergent nature of AI, coupled with its rapid evolution, AI-based applications face heightened risks.
elements.sent-2411-378: These risks must be regularly evaluated and addressed through comprehensive risk management strategies, ensuring that the AI systems remain robust and reliable over time.
elements.sent-2411-379: Vendor Management While the fundamentals of vendor management remain largely unchanged, the evaluation and onboarding of AI vendors require careful consideration.
elements.sent-2411-380: Organizations must assess whether new AI models utilize data responsibly, comply with enterprise contracts, and meet security standards before integration.
elements.sent-2411-381: Evaluation and On-boarding The evaluation of newer models and whether those utilize the data sent for training has to be considered before on-boarding the new AI models and technologies.
elements.sent-2411-382: Evaluating enterprise contracts and security compliance is also part of the evaluation and onboarding governance.
elements.sent-2411-383: Legal and Compliance Keeping abreast of the dynamic regulatory environment surrounding AI is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-384: Organizations must establish governance frameworks that ensure compliance with evolving legal requirements and guide the implementation of AI systems in line with these standards.
elements.sent-2411-385: Humanitarian governance focuses on the socio-technological impacts of AI systems, particularly on human-AI interactions, ethics, and organizational culture: Ethical Governance AI implementations must adhere to established ethical norms.
elements.sent-2411-386: This governance ensures that AI systems are developed and deployed in a manner that respects human rights, fairness, and transparency.
elements.sent-2411-387: Explainability Governance To foster trust and accountability, it is imperative that AI systems are explainable.
elements.sent-2411-388: This governance sets the required level of explainability for AI systems within the organization and mandates adherence to these standards across all AI-based solutions.
elements.sent-2411-389: Bias Governance Addressing biases in AI systems is critical.
elements.sent-2411-390: This governance requires the analysis of potential biases in AI implementations and the development of mitigation strategies to ensure fairness and equity in AI-driven decisions.
elements.sent-2411-391: Training and Education While training is an existing governance model, AI systems demand specialized training for teams involved in their development and use.
elements.sent-2411-392: This includes educating allied teams and customers on how to effectively interact with AI systems.
elements.sent-2411-393: Organizational Communication and Change Management Existing governance models for communication and change management must be adapted to meet the specific requirements of AI systems.
elements.sent-2411-394: Psycho-social Aspects of AI This includes addressing the psycho-social aspects of AI, such as managing staff and user anxieties through targeted training and support programs, thereby enhancing the adoption of AI technologies.
elements.sent-2411-395: Production governance ensures that AI systems are managed effectively once deployed, encompassing monitoring, disaster recovery, business continuity, incident management production support, etc. Data Governance Data governance for AI systems must be restructured to account for the dynamic nature of data that can modify AI models and outputs.
elements.sent-2411-396: This involves implementing stringent data quality controls and ensuring that the data feeding into AI models is reliable and accurate.
elements.sent-2411-397: Operational Governance While operational governance practices such as staff backups, rotation schedules, and incident management remain largely applicable to AI systems, they must be fine-tuned to accommodate the specific operational needs of AI technologies.
elements.sent-2411-398: Production Support and Assurance AI-based systems require specialized production support.
elements.sent-2411-399: Organizations must equip their production support teams with the necessary training and resources to handle the unique challenges posed by AI systems, ensuring that they can provide effective and timely support.
elements.sent-2411-400: This AI governance model is based on our learnings from this field study and is designed to provide a comprehensive framework that organizations can adopt to manage the complexities of AI deployment.
elements.sent-2411-401: By integrating these governance practices, organizations can ensure that their AI systems are not only effective but also ethical, compliant, and resilient in the face of evolving technological and regulatory landscapes.
elements.sent-2411-402: In our field study, we experimented with various prompt patterns \cite{white2023prompt and explored different aspects of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to develop best practices for building LLM-based systems.}
elements.sent-2411-403: The following recommendations are grounded in lessons learned from our build-test cycles and are designed to address key areas such as data governance, system design, user engagement, and ongoing evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-404: Prompt Structure: RAG allows LLMs to stay focused and minimize hallucinations by providing clear context through the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-405: We developed structured templates that enhance readability and flexibility during development.
elements.sent-2411-406: These templates also segment instructions into distinct sections, which is crucial for maintaining clarity.
elements.sent-2411-407: Incorporating a prefix section in the prompt helps define the LLM's persona and provides structural information, ensuring the model understands the organization of the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-408: Custom begin and end tags (e.g., <|im-start|>, <|im-end|>) facilitate easy parsing of the prompt's sections.
elements.sent-2411-409: Prompt Writing: Clarity and specificity in prompt instructions are vital for guiding the LLM effectively.
elements.sent-2411-410: For instance, instructions like "If there is not enough information, say 'I don't know'" or "Be brief in your answers" help the model deliver more accurate responses.
elements.sent-2411-411: Capitalizing key directives (e.g., "DO NOT," "ONLY") and numbering instructions can further emphasize critical points.
elements.sent-2411-412: When expecting output in a specific format (e.g., JSON), providing an example is beneficial.
elements.sent-2411-413: Additionally, using the function-calling feature of LLMs, such as in ChatGPT, can help ensure the output follows a particular pattern.
elements.sent-2411-414: Vector Databases: Experimenting with various vector databases and search mechanisms is crucial for optimizing RAG systems.
elements.sent-2411-415: We tested databases like Chroma, Pinecone, and Elastic Relevance Search Engine and compared different search strategies, including exact-match and semantic search, or a combination of both.
elements.sent-2411-416: Proper data governance involves selecting and managing these databases to ensure data accuracy, consistency, and privacy throughout the retrieval process.
elements.sent-2411-417: Establishing clear guidelines for data handling, storage, and retrieval is essential for maintaining the integrity of the information used by RAG systems.
elements.sent-2411-418: Modular Architecture: Given the rapidly evolving nature of AI technologies, designing the application in modular, decoupled components is advisable.
elements.sent-2411-419: This approach allows for easier updates and integration of new features without disrupting the entire system.
elements.sent-2411-420: Thorough planning of the architecture before building the prototype is essential.
elements.sent-2411-421: Extensive logging should be implemented to track which documents were retrieved for specific user queries and what responses were generated.
elements.sent-2411-422: This not only aids in debugging but also supports ongoing evaluation and improvement of the system.
elements.sent-2411-423: LLM Considerations: Selecting the right AI provider is critical.
elements.sent-2411-424: Enterprise accounts offer more reliable API access and support, which is particularly important for large-scale applications.
elements.sent-2411-425: It's beneficial to compare the capabilities of cloud-based LLMs (e.g., those offered by Google Vertex AI or Microsoft Azure OpenAI) with locally hosted, fine-tuned models on proprietary data.
elements.sent-2411-426: Each option has distinct advantages, depending on the business case, and should be evaluated in terms of scalability, cost, and data security.
elements.sent-2411-427: Human Factors: Engaging end users early in the development process is key to creating a system that meets their needs.
elements.sent-2411-428: Request users to provide a set of frequently used questions for quality testing and unit testing.
elements.sent-2411-429: This approach helps mitigate developers' bias and avoids tunnel vision, ensuring the system is tested against real-world scenarios that users will encounter.
elements.sent-2411-430: Continuous feedback from users should be integrated into the development cycle to refine the system's performance.
elements.sent-2411-431: Continuous Monitoring and Feedback: Implementing a robust system for ongoing evaluation is crucial for maintaining the effectiveness and relevance of the RAG system.
elements.sent-2411-432: This includes regular monitoring of the system's performance, user satisfaction, and compliance with data governance policies.
elements.sent-2411-433: Feedback loops should be established to capture insights from users and operational data, allowing for iterative improvements.
elements.sent-2411-434: Regular updates to the system based on these evaluations will help ensure that it continues to meet evolving user needs and technological advancements.
elements.sent-2411-435: By following these best practices that we contribute based on the insights from our field study, organizations can successfully implement RAG-based solutions using LLMs, ensuring that the technology is applied in a way that is effective, responsible, and aligned with industry standards and user expectations.
elements.sent-2411-436: This research and field study uncovered several technological, enterprise, and human factors that must be addressed as Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems continue to evolve.
elements.sent-2411-437: From a technological perspective, one major challenge encountered was the limitations associated with using external AI models like ChatGPT via APIs, such as rate limits, delayed responses, and token constraints.
elements.sent-2411-438: These limitations directly impact the performance and scalability of RAG applications, requiring careful consideration of model selection, token management, and response optimization.
elements.sent-2411-439: On the enterprise front, the use of external APIs for large language models (LLMs) necessitates a rigorous assessment of the proprietary information being exposed to these models, particularly in sensitive contexts where data security is paramount.
elements.sent-2411-440: Gaining stakeholder buy-in and securing budget allocations are critical steps in developing systems based on emerging technologies.
elements.sent-2411-441: This highlights the importance of robust AI governance frameworks, which we addressed in our study by proposing governance models that account for the unique requirements of AI systems, including prompt engineering, risk management, and legal compliance.
elements.sent-2411-442: Our pilot phase underscored the importance of managing user expectations for the successful deployment of AI-based applications.
elements.sent-2411-443: We found that user training is critical, particularly in question-and-answer interactions, where poorly framed questions can result in inaccurate or misleading responses.
elements.sent-2411-444: By educating users on how to interact effectively with AI systems, many of these issues can be mitigated, leading to improved overall system performance and user satisfaction.
elements.sent-2411-445: In addressing the challenges we faced, we encountered issues similar to those documented by Hevner et al. \cite{hevner2023research in their research on the design of human-artificial intelligence systems (HAIS).}
elements.sent-2411-446: These challenges included selecting appropriate technologies amid legal and compliance constraints, managing the complexity of integrating diverse data sources, and evaluating the effectiveness of LLM-based applications.
elements.sent-2411-447: Each of these issues required careful consideration and iterative problem-solving, such as modular design, user feedback through pre-surveys, and the strategic use of tools like TruLens \cite{pmlr-v176-datta22a , DeepEval \cite{Ip2024confident , LangSmith by LangChain \cite{Chase_LangChain_2022 , etc., for evaluation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-448: In addition, the implementation of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems in real-world scenarios necessitates a robust AI governance framework to ensure ethical, legal, and operational integrity.
elements.sent-2411-449: As AI technologies evolve, especially with advancements like RAG, governance models must adapt to address new challenges, including architectural design, risk management, and the socio-technical impacts on human-AI interactions.
elements.sent-2411-450: Our field study insights laid the groundwork for developing the proposed AI governance model tailored specifically to RAG systems, with a focus on key areas such as prompt engineering, data governance, and continuous evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-451: This governance model emphasizes the importance of maintaining transparency, ensuring ethical AI deployment, and managing risks associated with the rapid evolution of AI technologies.
elements.sent-2411-452: By adopting these governance practices, organizations can deploy RAG systems effectively while adhering to compliance standards and mitigating potential risks such as bias or misinformation.
elements.sent-2411-453: Integrating these insights into AI governance contributes to the broader discourse on responsible AI deployment and offers a practical framework for organizations aiming to harness the potential of RAG systems.
elements.sent-2411-454: Given the complex challenges revealed by our field study, it is essential for industry and academia to collaborate more closely to advance AI technologies in a manner that is both innovative and responsible.

elements.sent-2411-457: This study enhances the growing body of knowledge on RAG systems by providing a comprehensive analysis of the technical, organizational, and human challenges involved in their implementation.

elements.sent-2411-459: This study has highlighted the key challenges and opportunities associated with implementing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems using large language models (LLMs).
